edges.description: CSF dysregulation produces sustained elevation of intracranial pressure.
Elevated pressure injures the optic nerve head and threatens vision.
Elevated pressure produces the characteristic headache syndrome.
Raised pressure and altered intracranial compliance can engage trigeminovascular pain signaling, but mean pressure alone does not fully explain IIH headache.
Raised intracranial pressure can compress the transverse sinuses, worsening venous sinus stenosis and closing a positive feedback loop.
edges.target: Venous sinus stenosis and intracranial venous hypertension

edges.description: Metabolic and hormonal factors contribute to CSF and venous-pressure abnormalities.
Obesity-associated hormonal signalling acts on the choroid plexus epithelium to stimulate active CSF secretion.
Excess choroid plexus CSF secretion is the secretory component of dysregulated CSF dynamics.
Increased CSF secretion raises CSF volume and contributes directly to sustained elevation of intracranial pressure.
Elevated venous sinus pressure reduces the pressure gradient for CSF absorption across arachnoid granulations, worsening CSF dynamics.
edges.source: Venous sinus stenosis and intracranial venous hypertension

edges.description: Intracranial venous hypertension contributes directly to sustained elevation of intracranial pressure.
edges.source: Venous sinus stenosis and intracranial venous hypertension

edges.description: CGRP/neuropeptide activation of trigeminovascular pain pathways produces the migraine-like headache attacks that dominate IIH morbidity.

nodes.description: Acetazolamide is the best-studied medical therapy and improves visual outcomes when combined with a weight-reduction program in patients with mild visual loss.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of acetazolamide on visual function in patients with idiopathic intracranial hypertension and mild visual loss: the idiopathic intracranial hypertension treatment trial.
evidence.snippet: In patients with IIH and mild visual loss, the use of acetazolamide with a low-sodium weight-reduction diet compared with diet alone resulted in modest improvement in visual field function.

nodes.description: IIH headache is frequently migraine-like and is partly mediated by trigeminovascular neuropeptide signaling rather than by mean intracranial pressure alone. CGRP provocation triggers typical IIH headache attacks and increases ICP pulse amplitude, supporting a pressure-compliance and neuropeptide signaling branch for headache morbidity.

evidence.reference_title: Calcitonin gene-related peptide induces headache attacks in people with idiopathic intracranial hypertension.
evidence.snippet: CGRP reliably provoked typical IIH headache attacks (which have migraine-like features) and increased ICP pulse amplitude, as a measure of intracranial compliance, without altering mean pressure.

evidence.reference_title: Calcitonin gene-related peptide induces headache attacks in people with idiopathic intracranial hypertension.
evidence.snippet: These findings provide mechanistic support for CGRP involvement in headache attributed to IIH and justify prospective evaluation of CGRP pathway blockade in this population.

nodes.description: Obesity-associated metabolic and hormonal signalling is proposed to act on the choroid plexus epithelium to drive active, Na+/K+-ATPase-dependent cerebrospinal fluid secretion, the secretory arm of dysregulated CSF dynamics. Two complementary hormonal pathways are implicated: adipose 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1 activity, which regulates CSF secretion and tracks with intracranial pressure, and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor signalling on choroid plexus epithelium. Pharmacological proof-of-concept comes from 11-beta-HSD1 inhibition (AZD4017) and GLP-1 receptor agonism (exenatide) lowering intracranial pressure, although the 11-beta-HSD1 trial did not meet its primary endpoint.

evidence.reference_title: 11β-Hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1 inhibition in idiopathic intracranial hypertension: a double-blind randomized controlled trial.
evidence.snippet: The enzyme 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1 has been implicated in regulating cerebrospinal fluid secretion, and its activity is associated with alterations in intracranial pressure in idiopathic intracranial hypertension

evidence.reference_title: The effect of GLP-1RA exenatide on idiopathic intracranial hypertension: a randomized clinical trial.
evidence.snippet: Preclinical data have demonstrated a novel strategy to lower intracranial pressure using glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor signalling

evidence.reference_title: The effect of GLP-1RA exenatide on idiopathic intracranial hypertension: a randomized clinical trial.
evidence.snippet: Exenatide significantly and meaningfully lowered intracranial pressure at 2.5 h

nodes.description: The central pathophysiologic problem in IIH is dysregulation of CSF dynamics, leading to persistently elevated intracranial pressure in the absence of a mass lesion.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: Pathophysiology, diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: The pathophysiology involves dysregulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) dynamics and venous sinus pressure

nodes.description: Persistently elevated intracranial pressure causes the characteristic neuro-ophthalmologic and headache syndrome of pseudotumor cerebri.

evidence.snippet: Pseudotumor cerebri syndrome is a syndrome of increased cerebrospinal fluid pressure without ventriculomegaly, mass lesion, or meningeal abnormality.

nodes.description: Headache is the dominant symptomatic burden in pseudotumor cerebri.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: Pathophysiology, diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: The primary symptoms include headache, vision loss, and pulsatile tinnitus

nodes.description: Headache is the predominant symptomatic morbidity of pseudotumor cerebri. It reflects the elevated-pressure state, but current human provocation data support an additional CGRP-mediated trigeminovascular component that can vary independently of mean intracranial pressure.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension - Challenges and Pearls.
evidence.snippet: headache is the predominant morbidity in over 90%.

nodes.description: IIH strongly favors obese women of reproductive age, and metabolic and hormonal factors are thought to amplify CSF and venous-pressure dysregulation.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: Pathophysiology, diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: IIH demonstrates a strong predilection towards obese women of reproductive age

nodes.description: Papilledema is the hallmark neuro-ophthalmologic sign of raised intracranial pressure in IIH.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: Pathophysiology, diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is characterized by increased intracranial pressure, manifested by papilledema

nodes.description: Elevated intracranial pressure is transmitted to the subarachnoid space around the optic nerve, hindering axoplasmic transport in retinal ganglion cell axons and producing optic nerve head edema. Persistent papilledema can damage optic nerve fibers and threaten permanent visual loss if not recognized and treated.

evidence.reference_title: Papilledema: A review of etiology, pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management.
evidence.snippet: Papilledema is caused by transmission of elevated ICP to the subarachnoid space surrounding the optic nerve that hinders axoplasmic transport within ganglion cell axons.
Permanent visual loss occurs if undetected or untreated

nodes.description: Bilateral transverse and sigmoid sinus stenosis with a trans-stenosis pressure gradient raises intracranial venous pressure, impairing CSF absorption across the arachnoid granulations and contributing to raised intracranial pressure through a self-sustaining positive feedback loop. Whether venous sinus stenosis initiates the disease or is a pressure-dependent downstream consequence is debated, but relieving the gradient (for example by venous sinus stenting) can interrupt the loop.
nodes.id: Venous sinus stenosis and intracranial venous hypertension

evidence.reference_title: [Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: From physiopathological mechanisms to therapeutic decision].
evidence.snippet: bilateral stenosis of the transverse sinuses that plays a major role in the pathogenesis of the disease by creating a positive feedback loop that increases intracranial venous hypertension and contributes to clinical manifestations

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: Pathophysiology, diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: The pathophysiology involves dysregulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) dynamics and venous sinus pressure

evidence.reference_title: Transient resolution of venous sinus stenosis after high-volume lumbar puncture in a patient with idiopathic intracranial hypertension.
evidence.snippet: Stenosis of the transverse sinus (TS) and sigmoid sinus (SS), with a trans-stenosis pressure gradient, has been implicated in the pathophysiology of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH).

nodes.description: Visual dysfunction and permanent visual loss can occur if elevated intracranial pressure is not controlled.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: Pathophysiology, diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: The primary symptoms include headache, vision loss, and pulsatile tinnitus
Because of pressure on the optic nerve (papilledema), 86% have some degree of permanent visual loss and 10% develop severe visual loss

nodes.description: Weight reduction is a core disease-directed intervention in overweight patients with IIH.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension: Pathophysiology, diagnosis and management.
evidence.snippet: The Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension Treatment Trial, the first of its kind randomized controlled trial on IIH, provides class I evidence for treatment with weight loss and acetazolamide.
